headline: CMS content encryption arrives as pki.cms.encrypt and pki.cms.decrypt: EnvelopedData, AuthEnvelopedData, and EncryptedData with every RFC 5652 recipient type -- RSA-OAEP, ephemeral-static ECDH, X25519/X448, symmetric key-wrap, password, and post-quantum ML-KEM (RFC 9629/9936) -- and a single, oracle-free decryption verdict.
summary: pki.cms.encrypt(content, recipients, opts) produces a CMS EnvelopedData (CBC content), AuthEnvelopedData (AES-GCM content -- the authenticated default), or EncryptedData. One fresh content-encryption key is wrapped for every recipient, each arm auto-dispatched off the recipient's key material: an RSA certificate yields a ktri with RSAES-OAEP-SHA256 (PKCS#1 v1.5 is never emitted); an EC P-256/384/521 certificate a kari with ephemeral-static ECDH and the ANSI-X9.63 KDF; an X25519/X448 certificate a kari per RFC 8418 with HKDF; an ML-KEM-512/768/1024 certificate an ori/KEMRecipientInfo per RFC 9629 and RFC 9936 -- a post-quantum recipient is a registry row exactly like RSA, never a special case. A { password } recipient yields a pwri (PBKDF2 + the RFC 3211 PWRI-KEK); a { kek, kekId } recipient a kekri (AES key wrap). EncryptedData carries a raw { cek } or a PBES2 { password }. pki.cms.decrypt(input, keyMaterial, opts) selects the recipient the key material targets, acquires the content-encryption key through the matching arm (including PKCS#1 v1.5 decryption, accepted with the RFC 3218 implicit-rejection countermeasure but never emitted), and decrypts and authenticates the content, returning the plaintext with its content type, the recipient type and index, and an authenticated flag. Decryption is oracle-free by construction: every secret-dependent failure -- a bad key-wrap, a padding fault, an AES-KW integrity-check mismatch, a GCM tag mismatch, a PWRI check-byte mismatch -- collapses to the SINGLE uniform cms/decrypt-failed verdict, so a Bleichenbacher, EFAIL, or password-guessing oracle has no distinguishable signal. A CBC EnvelopedData surfaces authenticated:false in the verdict, making the unauthenticated-content caveat explicit; AES-GCM AuthEnvelopedData is the encrypt default. Both directions are cross-checked against openssl cms for every arm.

Added: pki.cms.encrypt(content, recipients, opts) produces a CMS EnvelopedData, AuthEnvelopedData (AES-GCM, the default), or EncryptedData. Recipients auto-dispatch off the certificate key: RSA -> ktri RSAES-OAEP-SHA256/384/512 (v1.5 never emitted); EC P-256/384/521 -> kari ephemeral-static ECDH with the ANSI-X9.63 KDF; X25519/X448 -> kari with HKDF (RFC 8418); ML-KEM-512/768/1024 -> ori/KEMRecipientInfo (RFC 9629/9936). A { password } recipient uses PBKDF2 + the RFC 3211 PWRI-KEK; a { kek, kekId } recipient uses AES key wrap. EncryptedData takes a raw { cek } or a PBES2 { password }. Content is AES-128/192/256-GCM or -CBC; the same fresh content-encryption key is wrapped for every recipient. Fail-closed with typed CmsError.

pki.cms.decrypt(input, keyMaterial, opts) decrypts an EnvelopedData / AuthEnvelopedData / EncryptedData (DER or PEM). It selects the recipient the key material { key, cert } / { password } / { kek } / { cek } targets, acquires the content-encryption key through the matching arm (RSA-OAEP or PKCS#1 v1.5 decrypt-only under the RFC 3218 implicit-rejection countermeasure, ECDH / X25519 / X448, AES key-unwrap, PBKDF2, ML-KEM decapsulation), and decrypts + authenticates the content -- returning { content, contentType, contentTypeName, recipientType, recipientIndex, contentEncryptionAlgorithm, authenticated }. Every secret-dependent failure collapses to one uniform cms/decrypt-failed verdict (Bleichenbacher / EFAIL / password-oracle freedom); a PBKDF2 iteration cap bounds password-based decryption work.

The WebCrypto engine gains ML-KEM key encapsulation (SubtleCrypto.encapsulateBits / decapsulateBits over FIPS 203) and the ANSI-X9.63 single-step key-derivation function (the X963KDF derive algorithm), the two primitives the post-quantum and elliptic-curve CMS recipient arms compose.
